data.title: IonQ jumps as CEO tells Barron's it wants to become 'Nvidia of quantum computing'
data.description:
• IonQ (NYSE:IONQ) shares jumped more than 14% on Thursday after CEO Niccolo de Masi told Barron's it wants to become the Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) of quantum computing.
  • “We’re in the business of quantum just like Nvidia and Broadcom are in the business of classical GPUs,” de Masi told the news outlet. “I believe IonQ will be the Nvidia player. There will be other people that copy us and follow us; they have always copied and followed us.”
  • “We feel good about our competitive positioning because, at the end of the day, we are trying to drive ecosystem,” de Masi added.
  • Shares of other quantum computing stocks also rose on Thursday, including Quantum Computing (QUBT), D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) and Arqit Quantum (ARQQ). IonQ shares are down 11% year-to-date, but have gained 344% over the past 12 months.
  • Investor interest in the sector surged last year after Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) unveiled its quantum computing chip, code-named Willow. Since then, Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN) have introduced quantum computing chips of their own.

data.title: Titan Machinery's shares surge as Q1 revenue jumps in Europe
data.description:
Titan Machinery (NASDAQ:TITN) surged as much as 17% Thursday morning before trimming its gain. The farm and construction equipment dealer posted fiscal first-quarter results that showed progress on inventory management and overseas growth.

The company swung to a net loss of $13.2 million, or $0.58 a share, for the quarter ended April 30, from net income of $9.4 million, or $0.41 a share, in the same period last year.

Revenue came in at $594.3 million, a 5.5% decline from $628.7 million a year ago.

Titan’s (NASDAQ:TITN) first-quarter earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization plummeted to $2.6 million from $30.9 million a year earlier.

“While headwinds persist across the agricultural sector, our team remains focused on continuing to execute upon our initiative to optimize inventory,” Bryan Knutson, chief executive of Titan (TITN), said in a statement.

Revenue driven by pre-sold equipment

Knutson cautioned that the better-than-expected revenue was primarily the result of timing on deliveries of pre-sold equipment, rather than any meaningful uptick in market demand. The agriculture sector, Titan’s (TITN) largest revenue source, remains under pressure due to weak commodity prices and tight farm margins.

The Agriculture segment posted $384.4 million in revenue, down 14% year over year, with a $12.8 million pre-tax loss. Titan (TITN) maintained its guidance for a full-year revenue decline of 20% to 25% in this core business.

Meanwhile, Construction segment revenue was flat at $72.1 million, with a small pre-tax loss of $4.2 million, while Australia saw relatively stable results despite foreign exchange headwinds.

European operations shine

The standout performer was Titan’s (TITMN) Europe segment, where revenue jumped nearly 44% year over year (adjusted for currency) to $93.9 million. That growth, driven largely by strong demand in Romania supported by EU stimulus funding, helped generate $4.7 million in pre-tax income, more than triple the year-ago result.

As a result, the company raised its full-year guidance for European revenue growth to 23% to 28%, up from a previous estimate of flat to 5%.

In contrast, the Australia segment recorded $44 million in revenue, nearly unchanged from last year, but guidance was lowered to a 20% to 25% annual decline amid local headwinds.

Inventory strategy gains ground

Titan’s (TITN) gross margin fell to 15.3% from 19.4% a year earlier, primarily because of aggressive pricing actions aimed at moving inventory in a sluggish retail environment, the company said. Gross profit was $90.9 million, down from $121.8 million.

Despite tighter margins, the company saw a sequential 15% decline in floorplan and other interest expenses, reflecting progress in inventory control, a central pillar of Titan’s (TITN) current strategy. Inventory levels remained flat at $1.1 billion quarter-over-quarter, while cash from operations improved to $6.2 million from a $32.4 million outflow a year ago.

Outlook unchanged

Titan (TITN) reaffirmed its full-year guidance of an adjusted loss between $1.25 and $2.00 a share. While management acknowledged continued uncertainty in the farm economy, including undefined government support programs, it expressed confidence in its operational focus and long-term positioning.

“Absent unique variables, the broader agriculture sector remains challenged in the near-term,” Knutson said. “The progression of our inventory reduction efforts remains core to our operating strategy to stabilize equipment margins and restore the business's earnings power.”

data.title: Ralph Lauren raises dividend by 10.6% to $0.9125
data.description:
• Ralph Lauren (NYSE:RL) declares $0.9125/share quarterly dividend, 10.6% increase from prior dividend of $0.8250.
  • Forward yield 1.31%
  • Payable July 11; for shareholders of record June 27; ex-div June 27.
  • See RL Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Unum to hike dividend by ~10%
data.description:
Unum Group (NYSE:UNM) announced on Thursday an authorization by the board to increase its quarterly dividend by ~10%, effective the third quarter of 2025.

The workplace benefits and services company last paid a $0.42/share quarterly dividend, which represents a payout of $1.68 on an annual basis.

The new rate of $0.46 per share brings the payout to $1.84 per share on an annual basis.

data.title: freenet AG reports Q1 results
data.description:
• freenet AG press release (OTCPK:FRTAF): Q1 Revenues increased moderately by 1.7% to EUR 604.4 million (prior year: EUR 594.5 million)
  • Adjusted EBITDA of EUR 126.1 million at the prior year's level (EUR 127.1 million)
  • Free cash flow increased by 1.7% to EUR 75.8 million (prior year: EUR 74.5 million)
  • Guidance for the full year 2025 confirmed.
  • The solid business development confirms the guidance for the Group to achieve an adjusted EBITDA of EUR 520 million to EUR 540 million by the end of the year (prior year: EUR 503.1 million) and a free cash flow of EUR 300 million to EUR 320 million (prior year: EUR 292.3 million).

data.title: Existing home sales unexpectedly edge down in April
data.description:
April U.S. existing home sales: -0.5% M/M to 4.00M vs. 4.130M consensus and 4.02M prior.

Median existing-home sales price increased 1.8% Y/Y to $414.0K. That compares with $403.7K in March, which was a 2.7% Y/Y increase.

Developing... check back for updates.

data.title: Solar stocks plunge after U.S. House passes Trump tax bill; Sunrun sinks 39%
data.description:
Solar company shares including Sunrun (NASDAQ:RUN) and SolarEdge Technologies (SEDG) fell sharply in early trading Thursday as a result of the tax and spending bill that narrowly passed the Republican-led House of Representatives, which could end numerous clean energy subsidies.

Enphase Energy (NASDAQ:ENPH) is the biggest loser on the S&P 500, -15.6%, followed by index components NextEra Energy (NEE) -7.6%, AES Corp. (AES) -5.7%, First Solar (FSLR) -3.7%.

Among other solar energy stocks: Sunrun (NASDAQ:RUN) -38.9%, SolarEdge Technologies (SEDG) -23.1%, Array Technologies (ARRY) -13.4%, Maxeon Solar Technologies (MAXN) -10.3%, Shoals Technologies (SHLS) -9.4%, FTC Solar (FTCI) -9.3%, Complete Solaria (SPWR) -7.4%, Canadian Solar (CSIQ) -6.8%, Fluence Energy (FLNC) -4.9%, JinkoSolar (JKS) -4%, Sunnova Energy (NOVA) -2.1%.

Among the changes included in the bill that still needs approval in the U.S. Senate was revised language that would end certain tax credits for solar and wind energy by 2028 instead of a slower phaseout through 2031; in the new proposed timeline, solar or wind projects must begin construction within 60 days of the bill's enactment and finish construction by year-end 2028 to remain eligible for tax credits.

The legislation also would remove the 30% federal tax credit for taxpayers who install solar rooftop systems.

While the industry anticipated the gradual phaseout of solar and wind tax credits, the new version of the bill accelerates the timeline, Raymond James analyst Pavel Molchanov told Reuters.

The bill is "worse than feared" for clean energy, Jeffries analyst said, but "we don't expect this to last into Senate draft."

data.title: JPMorgan's Dimon still sees plenty of economic risk on the horizon - report
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (JPM) Chairman and CEO Jamie Dimon said the "economy has been doing well," but he sees plenty of factors that could increase inflation and tamp down growth in months to come, according to a media interview on Thursday.

He points to the U.S. huge fiscal deficits, that the Federal Reserve's monetary tightening path hasn't yet ended, and serious geopolitical issues. "There are inflationary factors out there," Dimon said.

Meanwhile, he thinks the Fed is taking the right approach in navigating the environment. "I think they're doing the right thing to kind of wait and see before they decide," he said in an interview on Bloomberg Television.

"They have to react to reality," Dimon said. "That's not a criticism... They're data dependent. They have to wait and see exactly what happens and then do the appropriate thing."

While the U.S. economy has "effectively been in a soft landing," he warns that "that doesn't tell you what the future is going to be."

The equity indexes are mixed in Thursday trading. The Nasdaq gained 0.2%, the S&P and Dow each slipped ~0.1%.

data.title: U.S. PMI Composite rises more than expected in May.
data.description: The S&P Global U.S. Composite PMI rose to 52.2 in May's flash estimate, exceeding the 49.9 consensus, from 50.2 in April.
